## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Study-gated migration. One file. A cheap spike decides the repair shape; do
  not commit to a form before it. The reliability spine is a verdict baseline,
  not the pace boundary.
  
  ## Goal
  
  Theurge's fast-suite cases in `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs` exercise
  contract surfaces, not module internals. The bypass — hardcoded module source
  paths, replicated kindle chains, direct `z*` calls — is gone from the
  regime-validation family. Every case's verdict is unchanged.
  
  ## Starting facts (from the ₣BJ survey — do not re-derive)
  
  - `rbtdrf_fast.rs` is the sole bypass-heavy file. `rbtdrk_canonical.rs`,
    `rbtdro_onboarding.rs`, `rbtdrp_pristine.rs`, `rbtdrf_handbook.rs` already
    route through tabtargets.
  - The tabtarget-invocation helpers `rbtdrf_run_tt` and `rbtdrf_run_tt_neg`
    already exist in `rbtdrf_fast.rs` — no minting needed.
  - The regime-smoke and dockerfile-hygiene families already use tabtargets — no
    work there.
  - Candidate surfaces for regime-validation: `rbw-rrv`, `rbw-rvv`, `rbw-rnv`.
  
  ## Step gate — settle this first
  
  The regime-validation negatives feed deliberately-bad regime state. Determine
  whether a validate tabtarget can be pointed at a *staged malformed regime*
  (injectable regime dir / moorings-location override) so a negative runs through
  `rbtdrf_run_tt_neg`.
  
  - **Yes** → regime-validation is tabtarget-coverable; migration is mechanical.
    The RBRR / RBRV / RBRN negatives break into independent commits — notch each
    sub-family separately, baseline-diffing after each.
  - **No** → these need a declared public/test entry point (sketch
    `<prefix>_t_validate <regime>`), NOT a test-only tabtarget. If this path is
    forced and the work is substantial, cantle it out.
  
  ## enrollment-validation disposition
  
  Pure-utility — BUV type checks via the public `buv_vet`, which is itself a
  legitimate contract surface. No natural tabtarget; do NOT invent one. The only
  mess is path-coupling and any `z*` reach (e.g. enrollment reset). Replace those
  with a stable reference / declared entry where warranted; confirm-and-leave is
  an acceptable outcome.
  
  ## Reliability discipline
  
  - Capture a verdict baseline (run the fast suite, record per-case Pass/Fail)
    BEFORE any edit. The post-migration verdict set must match exactly.
  - A migrated negative must fail for the SAME reason, not merely fail.
    Spot-check the diagnostic, not just the exit status.
  - Refactor-safety proof: temporarily move or rename a sourced module; migrated
    cases must still pass, or fail with a contract-level diagnostic — never "no
    such file."
  
  ## Out of scope
  
  - New theurge cases. Migration only.
  - New tabtargets purely for testing.
  - BUK testbenches in `Tools/buk/`.
  - Engine-helper redesign beyond what migration demands.
  
  ## References
  
  - `rbtdri_invocation.rs` header — documented principle "theurge invokes bottle
    operations exclusively through tabtargets."
  - ₢BBABJ design conversation — original mess characterization and principles.

## Paddock: rbk-14-mvp-testing-simplification

## Shape

Theurge — the crucible test orchestrator — should be a clean *consumer* of
operator tabtargets, never an owner of commands and never a reacher into module
internals. This heat closes the two places where it still violates that.

**1. Access-probe relocation.** Theurge currently owns the `rbtd-ap` colophon
family (the credential access probes), the only colophon it owns. This work
retires that family into four operator credential-check tabtargets
(`rbw-acg/acr/acd/acp`) dispatched by `rbw_workbench`, leaving theurge owning
zero colophons. The `rbgv_AccessProbe.sh` implementation is unchanged — only
ownership and dispatch move. Mechanical; the docket carries the full tabtarget
mapping and done-when.

**2. Contract-surface migration.** Theurge's fast-suite validation cases
bypass tabtargets — hardcoding module source paths, replicating kindle chains,
calling `z`-private functions. This work moves them onto contract surfaces.

## Locked decisions / findings (from grooming survey, 260520)

- **Sequencing behind ₣BK.** Both work items sit inside ₣BK's moorings-cutover
  blast radius (the same `rbtd-ap` tabtargets, `rbtdrp_pristine.rs`, and
  `rbtdrf_fast.rs` that ₣BK's filesystem-rename and literal-sweep touch). This
  heat mounts only *after* ₣BK lands. Doing it after — not concurrently —
  removes all concurrent-edit risk; and both work items shrink ₣BK's surface
  (the access-probe work deletes four tabtargets; the migration work strips the
  hardcoded `.rbk/` path literals that ₣BK's literal-sweep names as its
  canonical example). Operator holds this schedule.

- **The trampoline form is settled.** ₣BK's tabtarget trampoline already landed.
  New operator tabtargets are the one-line `exec .../z-launcher.sh rbml_rbw
  "${0##*/}" "${@}"` form — they carry no `.buk`/`.rbk` literal and are immune
  to ₣BK's later launcher-path flip.

- **The migration is one file.** `rbtdrf_fast.rs` is the sole bypass-heavy
  surface; the sibling theurge test files already route through tabtargets. The
  `rbtdrf_run_tt` / `rbtdrf_run_tt_neg` helpers already exist. regime-smoke and
  dockerfile-hygiene families already use tabtargets.

- **The work splits by family, not by pace.** regime-validation is the real
  migration target (candidate surfaces `rbw-rrv/rvv/rnv`). enrollment-validation
  is pure-utility (BUV type checks via public `buv_vet`) — leave it, do not
  invent a test-only tabtarget for it. Kept as a single study-gated pace;
  decompose only if the crux below forces invented test-API.

- **Pivotal unknown (settle first at mount).** Whether a validate tabtarget can
  be pointed at a *staged malformed regime* decides whether regime-validation
  negatives are tabtarget-coverable (mechanical) or need a declared
  `*_t_validate` entry point (a design sub-task).

- **Reliability spine.** A pre-migration verdict baseline (per-case Pass/Fail
  across the fast suite) is the safety net — the post-migration verdict set must
  match exactly, and migrated negatives must fail for the *same reason*, not
  merely fail.

## Done looks like

Theurge owns no colophons; the four credential checks are operator tabtargets.
`rbtdrf_fast.rs`'s regime-validation family exercises tabtargets (or declared
test entry points), with no hardcoded module paths or `z`-private reach. Fast,
complete, and gauntlet suites pass with verdicts matching the pre-migration
baseline.

## References

- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrf_fast.rs` — migration surface
- `Tools/rbk/rbgv_AccessProbe.sh` — access-probe implementation (unchanged)
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/rbte_cli.sh`, `rbte_engine.sh` — theurge colophon ownership to strip
- `Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh`, `rbw_workbench.sh` — operator dispatch enrollment
- `Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrm_manifest.rs` — fixture-to-colophon dependency tables
- `rbtdri_invocation.rs` header — "theurge invokes bottle operations exclusively through tabtargets"

# Mounting ₢BJAAB — theurge-contract-surface-migration

**Goal in one sentence:** Move theurge's regime-validation fast-suite cases in `rbtdrf_fast.rs` off their tabtarget-bypass (hardcoded module source paths, replicated kindle chains, direct `z*` calls) and onto contract surfaces — with every case's verdict provably unchanged.

**Heat context** — `rbk-14-mvp-testing-simplification` (₣BJ): theurge should be a clean *consumer* of operator tabtargets, never an owner of commands nor a reacher into module internals. Pace 1 (₢BJAAA) landed the access-probe relocation — theurge now owns zero colophons. This pace closes the second violation: the internal-reaching fast-suite cases. 38	/// Sub-assertion within a case: run bash snippet, check exit code.
39	struct RbtdrfSub {
40	    label: &'static str,
41	    setup: &'static str,
42	    command: &'static str,
43	    expect_ok: bool,
44	}
45	
46	impl RbtdrfSub {
47	    const fn ok(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str) -> Self {
48	        Self { label, setup, command: "buv_vet \"TEST\"", expect_ok: true }
49	    }
50	    const fn fatal(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str) -> Self {
51	        Self { label, setup, command: "buv_vet \"TEST\"", expect_ok: false }
52	    }
53	    const fn ok_cmd(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str, command: &'static str) -> Self {
54	        Self { label, setup, command, expect_ok: true }
55	    }
56	    const fn fatal_cmd(label: &'static str, setup: &'static str, command: &'static str) -> Self {
57	        Self { label, setup, command, expect_ok: false }
58	    }
59	}

61	/// Run a bash script, return (exit_code, stdout, stderr). Saves traces to case dir.
62	fn rbtdrf_run_bash(
63	    project_root: &Path,
64	    script: &str,
65	    dir: &Path,
66	    trace_prefix: &str,
67	) -> Result<(i32, String, String), String> {
68	    let output = Command::new("bash")
69	        .arg("-c")
70	        .arg(script)
71	        .current_dir(project_root)
72	        .output()
73	        .map_err(|e| format!("bash execution failed: {}", e))?;
74	
75	    let stdout = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stdout).into_owned();
76	    let stderr = String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr).into_owned();
77	    let code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1);
78	
79	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-script.sh", trace_prefix)), script);
80	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stdout.txt", trace_prefix)), &stdout);
81	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stderr.txt", trace_prefix)), &stderr);
82	
83	    Ok((code, stdout, stderr))
84	}

86	/// Run enrollment-validation sub-assertions against BUV.
87	fn rbtdrf_run_ev(
88	    dir: &Path,
89	    enrollment: &str,
90	    subs: &[RbtdrfSub],
91	) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
92	    let root = match std::env::current_dir() {
93	        Ok(r) => r,
94	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("cannot get cwd: {}", e)),
95	    };
96	    let buv = root.join("Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh");
97	
98	    for (i, sub) in subs.iter().enumerate() {
99	        let script = format!(
100	            "set -euo pipefail\nsource '{}'\nzbuv_kindle\nzbuv_reset_enrollment\n{}\n{}\n{}",
101	            rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&buv),
102	            enrollment,
103	            sub.setup,
104	            sub.command,
105	        );
106	
107	        match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script, dir, &format!("sub-{}", i)) {
108	            Ok((code, _, _)) => {
109	                let ok = code == 0;
110	                if ok != sub.expect_ok {
111	                    return if sub.expect_ok {
112	                        rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected ok, got exit {}", sub.label, code))
113	                    } else {
114	                        rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected failure, got exit 0", sub.label))
115	                    };
116	                }
117	            }
118	            Err(e) => {
119	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: {}", sub.label, e));
120	            }
121	        }
122	    }
123	    rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
124	}

178	/// Run a tabtarget and check exit 0.
179	fn rbtdrf_run_tt(
180	    project_root: &Path,
181	    colophon: &str,
182	    args: &[&str],
183	    dir: &Path,
184	    label: &str,
185	) -> Result<(), String> {
186	    let tt = rbtdri_find_tabtarget_global(project_root, colophon)?;
187	    let output = Command::new(&tt)
188	        .args(args)
189	        .current_dir(project_root)
190	        .output()
191	        .map_err(|e| format!("{}: failed to run {}: {}", label, tt.display(), e))?;
192	
193	    let code = output.status.code().unwrap_or(-1);
194	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stdout.txt", label)), &output.stdout);
195	    let _ = std::fs::write(dir.join(format!("{}-stderr.txt", label)), &output.stderr);
196	
197	    if code != 0 {
198	        return Err(format!(
199	            "{}: {} exited {} — {}",
200	            label,
201	            colophon,
202	            code,
203	            String::from_utf8_lossy(&output.stderr),
204	        ));
205	    }
206	    Ok(())
207	}

496	    "sha256:abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789";
497	
498	fn rbtdrf_ev_odref_valid(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
499	    let root = match std::env::current_dir() {
500	        Ok(r) => r,
501	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("cannot get cwd: {}", e)),
502	    };
503	    let buv = root.join("Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh");
504	    let enrollment = format!(
505	        "set -euo pipefail\nsource '{}'\nzbuv_kindle\nzbuv_reset_enrollment\n\
506	         buv_regime_enroll \"TEST\"\nbuv_group_enroll \"References\"\n\
507	         buv_odref_enroll TEST_IMAGE \"Container image\"",
508	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&buv),
509	    );
510	    let d = RBTDRF_VALID_DIGEST;
511	
512	    let subs: &[(&str, &str)] = &[
513	        ("standard registry", &format!("docker.io/library/alpine@{}", d)),
514	        ("multi-level repo", &format!("us-central1-docker.pkg.dev/my-proj/my-repo/tool@{}", d)),
515	        ("registry with port", &format!("registry.local:5000/myimage@{}", d)),
516	    ];
517	
518	    for (i, (label, image)) in subs.iter().enumerate() {
519	        let script = format!("{}\nexport TEST_IMAGE=\"{}\"\nbuv_vet \"TEST\"", enrollment, image);
520	        match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script, dir, &format!("sub-{}", i)) {
521	            Ok((0, _, _)) => {}
522	            Ok((code, _, _)) => {
523	                return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: expected ok, got exit {}", label, code));
524	            }
525	            Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("{}: {}", label, e)),
526	        }
527	    }
528	    rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
529	}

761	fn rbtdrf_ev_multiscope(dir: &Path) -> rbtdre_Verdict {
762	    let root = match std::env::current_dir() {
763	        Ok(r) => r,
764	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("cannot get cwd: {}", e)),
765	    };
766	    let buv = root.join("Tools/buk/buv_validation.sh");
767	
768	    // Two-scope enrollment: ALPHA and BETA
769	    let enrollment = format!(
770	        "set -euo pipefail\nsource '{}'\nzbuv_kindle\nzbuv_reset_enrollment\n\
771	         buv_regime_enroll \"ALPHA\"\nbuv_group_enroll \"Alpha Vars\"\n\
772	         buv_bool_enroll TEST_ALPHA_FLAG \"Alpha flag\"\n\
773	         buv_regime_enroll \"BETA\"\nbuv_group_enroll \"Beta Vars\"\n\
774	         buv_bool_enroll TEST_BETA_FLAG \"Beta flag\"\n\
775	         export TEST_ALPHA_FLAG=\"1\"\nexport TEST_BETA_FLAG=\"bad\"\n",
776	        rbtdrx_native_to_posix(&buv),
777	    );
778	
779	    // Sub 1: vet ALPHA passes (BETA is bad but not in scope)
780	    let script1 = format!("{}buv_vet \"ALPHA\"", enrollment);
781	    match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script1, dir, "sub-0") {
782	        Ok((code, _, _)) if code == 0 => {}
783	        Ok((code, _, _)) => {
784	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("alpha scope: expected ok, got exit {}", code));
785	        }
786	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("alpha scope: {}", e)),
787	    }
788	
789	    // Sub 2: vet BETA fails
790	    let script2 = format!("{}buv_vet \"BETA\"", enrollment);
791	    match rbtdrf_run_bash(&root, &script2, dir, "sub-1") {
792	        Ok((code, _, _)) if code != 0 => {}
793	        Ok((_, _, _)) => {
794	            return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail("beta scope: expected failure, got exit 0".to_string());
795	        }
796	        Err(e) => return rbtdre_Verdict::Fail(format!("beta scope: {}", e)),
797	    }
798	
799	    rbtdre_Verdict::Pass
800	}

## Stopping the loop — the pace premise is blocked by regime state

I ran the verdict baseline first (the reliability spine for ₢BJAAB). It came back **red, for a reason that has nothing to do with the migration code** — so I've halted rather than capture a contaminated baseline or barrel into edits.

### What I found

`tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` → enrollment-validation 47/47 pass, then **regime-validation 25/26 — `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels` FAILED**, suite aborts (regime-smoke never runs).

The diagnostic is unambiguous:

```
ERROR: RBRV_RELIQUARY: RBRV_RELIQUARY must not be empty
```

And the cause is regime state, not code — **every** vessel has an empty reliquary:

| Vessel | `RBRV_RELIQUARY` |
|--------|------------------|
| all 9 (`rbev-bottle-*`, `rbev-busybox`, `rbev-graft-demo`, `rbev-sentry-deb-tether`) | *(empty)* |

`rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels` iterates every vessel in `RBRR_VESSEL_DIR` and runs `zrbrv_enforce`; an empty `RBRV_RELIQUARY` is a fatal, so the case fails.

### Why this happened

HEAD is the access-probe commit (`edeaad76c`), which sits just above `5c7a4e543 Marshal Zero — release qualification reset`. That commit's own message claims "fast 107/107" — **but it was run live against a freshly-levied, yoked depot** (`canest2bhl100021`). Yoking (`rbw-dY`) writes `RBRV_RELIQUARY` into `rbrv.env`, and those values are **not in the committed tree**. So the suite is green live (post-yoke) and red on any clean checkout. That's exactly the kind of "positive case silently depends on mutable operator regime state" fragility this heat is about — but repairing it is its own decision, not part of ₢BJAAB's mechanical migration.

### Why I stopped instead of self-pacing onward

- A meaningful verdict baseline is impossible in this state — the positive case is red regardless of code, so "post-migration verdicts must match exactly" has no clean reference.
- The repair is **operator territory and out of this pace's scope**: it's either yoke a reliquary into the vessels (`rbw-dY <stamp>` — needs a live director/depot, credentials), or decide the case should be independent of yoked state. I won't edit `rbrv.env` reliquary values myself (regime state I didn't create), nor run a credentialed yoke autonomously.
- Rescheduling a retry would be pointless — nothing changes without your action. So I've ended the loop.
- Worth flagging against the paddock's locked sequencing: ₣BK (moorings-cutover, **9/10 — not landed**) is the gate this pace is supposed to mount behind.

### Your call

1. **Yoke a reliquary** into the vessels, then I re-baseline and proceed — or
2. **Treat 106-pass/1-fail as the accepted baseline** for this state (I'd require post-migration to reproduce exactly that, including the same failure-reason) — or
3. **Defer ₢BJAAB** until ₣BK lands / the regime is in a yoked state — or
4. Fold "make `rbrv_all_vessels` independent of operator-yoked state" into scope (it's adjacent to this heat's theme, but currently out of scope per the docket).

Which way do you want to go?

local z_np_tmp=""
  for z_np in "${RBCC_moorings_dir}"/*/rbrn.env; do
    test -f "${z_np}" || continue
    z_np_tmp="${z_np}.tmp"
    while IFS= read -r z_line; do
      case "${z_line}" in
        RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK=*)  printf '%s\n' "RBRN_SENTRY_HALLMARK=" ;;
        RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK=*)  printf '%s\n' "RBRN_BOTTLE_HALLMARK=" ;;
        *)                           printf '%s\n' "${z_line}"                  ;;
      esac
    done < "${z_np}" > "${z_np_tmp}" && mv "${z_np_tmp}" "${z_np}"
    buh_line "  Blanked hallmarks: ${z_np}"
  done

  # Blank depot-scoped fields in all vessel regime files.
  # RBRV_RELIQUARY references a reliquary inscribed to the prior depot's GAR.
  # RBRV_IMAGE_*_ANCHOR references enshrined base images in the prior depot's GAR.
  # Both become stale after depot change — onboarding requires inscribe + enshrine.
  if test -n "${z_vessel_dir}" && test -d "${z_vessel_dir}"; then
    local z_vr=""
    local z_vr_tmp=""
    for z_vr in "${z_vessel_dir}"/*/rbrv.env; do
      test -f "${z_vr}" || continue
      z_vr_tmp="${z_vr}.tmp"
      while IFS= read -r z_line; do
        case "${z_line}" in
          RBRV_RELIQUARY=*)       printf '%s\n' "RBRV_RELIQUARY="       ;;
          RBRV_IMAGE_*_ANCHOR=*)  printf '%s\n' "${z_line%%=*}="        ;;
          *)                      printf '%s\n' "${z_line}"             ;;
        esac
      done < "${z_vr}" > "${z_vr_tmp}" && mv "${z_vr_tmp}" "${z_vr}"
      buh_line "  Blanked depot-scoped fields: ${z_vr}"
    done
  fi

  buh_line "  Zero complete: ${z_rbrr}"
  buh_line "                 ${z_rbrd}"
  buh_e

  # Auto-commit the in-tree mutations so post-zero state is captured as a single
  # commit. RBRA deletions (outside repo) are not staged.
  buc_step "Committing marshal-zero state"
  git add "${z_rbrr}" || buc_die "Failed to stage RBRR file"
  git add "${z_rbrd}" || buc_die "Failed to stage RBRD file"

content: Did we create a depot in this chat?  Begging your pardon, I'm juggling several chats.

content.text:
That check settles it. Look at `rblm_cli.sh:206` — Marshal Zero **deliberately blanks** `RBRV_RELIQUARY=`:

```bash
RBRV_RELIQUARY=*)  printf '%s\n' "RBRV_RELIQUARY="  ;;
```
> "Both become stale after depot change — onboarding requires inscribe + enshrine."

So the empty reliquary isn't damage — it's Marshal Zero working as designed, because the destroyed depot's reliquary is now stale.

## Answering directly: no, not without a depot

A reliquary lives **inside a depot's GAR**. The lifecycle is `rbw-dL` (levy depot) → `rbw-dI` (inscribe reliquary) → `rbw-dY` (yoke stamp into vessels). And `rbw-dY` *pre-validates the stamp against GAR before writing* (`rbfl_FoundryLedger.sh`). With all depots destroyed there's no GAR to inscribe into or validate against — so there's no depot-less "just create a reliquary." A real one means re-levying a depot: credentialed and billable.

The *only* depot-less thing possible is faking it: `RBRV_RELIQUARY` is validated merely as a non-empty ≤14-char datestamp string (`r260324193326` shape), so I could write a placeholder like `r260521000000` into all 9 vessels and the fast suite would go green.

**I'd advise against that**, for two reasons:
1. It directly contradicts what Marshal Zero just deliberately did — re-introducing the stale value it intentionally cleared.
2. It's committed state that *lies*: a stamp with no images behind it. Anyone who later tries to summon hits a phantom reliquary.

## The real finding

The red fast suite is the useful signal here: **`rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels` is mis-tiered.** It sits in the "fast / dependency-free" tier (CLAUDE.md: "Dependencies: None") but actually requires a yoked reliquary — i.e., a live depot. That's why "107/107" only ever holds transiently mid-qualification with a levied depot, and never from a clean checkout. This is *exactly* the "theurge test reaches into mutable operator state" fragility ₣BJ exists to kill.

## My recommendation

Given depots are gone, a clean baseline for ₢BJAAB genuinely cannot exist right now without re-levying. So:

- **If you're heading into release qualification anyway** (₣BB is at 55/63) — re-levy a depot and let the normal inscribe+yoke restore a real reliquary; then I baseline and proceed. Don't do it *just* to unblock me.
- **If not** — the durable move is to make `rbrv_all_vessels` tier-correct (skip/condition on empty reliquary, or relocate it out of fast) so the fast tier is genuinely dependency-free. That's ₣BJ's own theme and arguably belongs in this heat. I'd lean here.
- **What I would not do**: write a placeholder stamp to paper over it.

Which way — are you about to re-levy for qualification, or should I treat the mis-tiering as the thing to fix?

## This changes the answer to your original question — yes, I can

A *real* reliquary is creatable right now, because the depot is live. The path is two director operations:

1. **`tt/rbw-dI.DirectorInscribesReliquary.sh`** — mirrors the tool images from upstream into the live depot's GAR under a fresh datestamped namespace, emitting a reliquary stamp (`rYYMMDDHHMMSS`).
2. **`tt/rbw-dY.DirectorYokesReliquaryAllVessels.sh <stamp>`** — validates that stamp against GAR, then rewrites `RBRV_RELIQUARY` across all 9 vessels.

That greens `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels` legitimately — no placeholder, no lying state — and gives me the clean fast-suite baseline ₢BJAAB needs.

Two caveats before I run anything:

- These are **director, credentialed, and outward-facing**: inscribe pushes images into GAR (billable, and it's a real cloud mutation). Yoke also commits the rewritten `rbrv.env` files into the repo.
- I assume director credentials are installed. If not, `rbw-dI` will fail fast and I'll report.

Want me to run `rbw-dI` then `rbw-dY` now? (I'll do them one at a time and show you the stamp before yoking.)
I'll run the inscribe first and surface the reliquary stamp before yoking.

`rbtdrf_rv_rbrn_all_nameplates` is the **last** case in regime-validation (line 1619). The fixture aborts at the first failing case, so each fix exposes the next — but this is the final landmine in this family; clearing it lets regime-validation complete and regime-smoke (9 cases, which look genuinely dependency-free) finally run.

## Where things stand

| Item | State |
|------|-------|
| Reliquary `r260521024212` | ✅ inscribed + yoked into 9 vessels (real, validated against GAR) |
| `rbtdrf_rv_rbrv_all_vessels` | ✅ now passes |
| `rbtdrf_rv_rbrn_all_nameplates` | ❌ all 5 nameplates have empty hallmarks |
| 9 × `rbrv.env` (yoke edits) | ⚠️ uncommitted |

The reliquary you asked for is **done**. The hallmark failure is the *same Marshal-Zero pattern* — and it forces a real decision, because restoring hallmarks is an order of magnitude heavier than the reliquary was.

The reliquary was one cheap Cloud Build into a live depot. Hallmarks mean **ordaining** sentry+bottle images across 5 nameplates — potentially ~6–10 Cloud Builds, billable, tens of minutes. And here's the rub: this is the second "fast / dependency-free" positive (per CLAUDE.md) that actually requires live depot-scoped state Marshal Zero deliberately blanks. Restoring it greens the suite *today*, but the next Marshal Zero (which ₣BB's release-qualification reset does routinely) re-reds it. That Sisyphean loop is precisely the mis-tiering ₣BJ exists to kill.
